name: Maya Chen
title: Director, Compliance Operations
q1_search_analyze: Mostly SharePoint + network drives + eDiscovery pulls. I request exports from Legal/IT, then review PDFs in Adobe with keyword search and manual tagging in Excel.
q1_frustrating: Slow turnaround for exports, duplicates everywhere, no consistent naming, and it’s hard to prove we reviewed the *right* version. Cross-document search is weak unless we do another export.
q2_ai_privacy: Leadership is interested, but the default stance is 'assume leakage risk.' We’re exploring internal copilots with strict logging and retention controls.
q3_policies_chatgpt: Yes—explicitly prohibited to paste internal docs into public AI tools. Security does periodic reminders and we’ve had a couple near-miss incidents.
q4_adopt_private_ai: If it’s deployed in our tenant/VPC, supports role-based access, audit logs, and a clear retention model. Also needs a straightforward procurement/security review path.
q5_approver: Security, Legal, Compliance, and our Risk committee. IT would own implementation.
q6_vendor_comfort: Clear architecture, third-party security reports, data isolation guarantees, configurable retention, strong admin controls, and references from similar regulated firms.

name: Jordan Patel
title: IT Manager, Corporate Systems
q1_search_analyze: Shared drives + M365 search. Teams chats often contain the latest 'real' info. For analysis we export to CSV/PDF and people summarize manually.
q1_frustrating: People can’t find things reliably. Search results change, permissions hide key docs, and tribal knowledge lives in Teams. Everyone recreates the same summaries.
q2_ai_privacy: We’re cautiously experimenting. We want something that improves search and summarization without creating a new security headache.
q3_policies_chatgpt: We have guidance: 'don’t upload proprietary documents.' It’s not enforced consistently. Some teams use ChatGPT anyway for drafts.
q4_adopt_private_ai: If it integrates with M365 permissions, is simple to administer, and doesn’t require a huge data engineering project. A pilot that shows time saved would help.
q5_approver: IT + Security lead + VP Operations. Procurement for contracts.
q6_vendor_comfort: Easy SSO, least-privilege by default, transparent pricing, and the ability to prove it’s respecting permissions. Also an off switch and good support.

name: Elena Ruiz
title: Senior Counsel, Litigation & Investigations
q1_search_analyze: We do legal holds, then use eDiscovery platforms for search, dedup, and review workflows. Investigations often involve emails, Slack/Teams exports, and policy documents.
q1_frustrating: Review is expensive and slow. Non-lawyers can’t easily self-serve. Summaries risk missing nuance, and privilege considerations make sharing analysis difficult.
q2_ai_privacy: Strong interest, but concerns are higher than usual because of PHI and litigation risk. We need defensibility: logs, reproducibility, and controls.
q3_policies_chatgpt: Strictly prohibited for internal docs or PHI. We treat public AI as an external disclosure channel.
q4_adopt_private_ai: Defensible workflows: citations to source passages, immutable audit logs, retention controls, and the ability to lock models/config for a matter. Also needs PHI-safe handling.
q5_approver: General Counsel, Chief Privacy Officer, Security, and Compliance. Outside counsel may weigh in.
q6_vendor_comfort: Defensibility is the key: explainability, traceable outputs, matter-based access, and strong contractual terms. A vendor that understands legal review wins.

name: Sam O’Neill
title: Head of Knowledge Management
q1_search_analyze: Confluence + Google Drive + past proposals in shared folders. We maintain curated collections and templates, plus a lightweight tagging scheme.
q1_frustrating: Everyone wants a single search bar, but content is scattered. Good stuff is buried in old proposals. Tagging is inconsistent and curation doesn’t scale.
q2_ai_privacy: We’re optimistic. We want AI for internal reuse and faster proposal drafting, but we can’t risk leaking client info.
q3_policies_chatgpt: Allowed for non-client content. For client material, it’s not allowed unless it’s in an approved private system.
q4_adopt_private_ai: A system that respects doc permissions, supports curated 'gold' sources, and can generate answers with citations. Also needs easy redaction controls for client content.
q5_approver: CIO + Security + KM + a client risk committee.
q6_vendor_comfort: Permission-aware retrieval, citation-first answers, admin tooling for collections, and clear boundaries between client and internal knowledge. Proof of controls matters more than fancy demos.

name: Priya Narang
title: VP, Data & Analytics
q1_search_analyze: Notion + Slack + GitHub + ticketing. For analysis we build small datasets from exports and run quick SQL/Python notebooks to summarize themes.
q1_frustrating: Context is fragmented across tools. Answers depend on who you ask. Slack threads disappear, and people don’t trust search enough to stop asking in chat.
q2_ai_privacy: We want AI to speed up internal Q&A, but we also want governance. We’re trying to avoid a shadow-AI culture.
q3_policies_chatgpt: Guidelines exist: no secrets, no customer data. Enforcement is light; teams differ.
q4_adopt_private_ai: If it plugs into our stack quickly, supports SSO, has sane pricing, and lets us define what sources are allowed. Also needs monitoring for risky prompts.
q5_approver: Me + Security + Engineering leadership. If it touches customer data, Legal too.
q6_vendor_comfort: Fast time-to-value, good connectors, admin controls, usage analytics, and the ability to sandbox. Also: strong stance on training/no-training on our data.
